title: A 500-Mile Road Trip for a Campus Show: Inside Turning Point’s Wild Idaho Finale
summary: Would you drive over 500 miles to see your favorite podcasters? That’s what Chloe Moes and Marissa Aten did. Moes, 20, came from California, and Aten, 23, from Nevada, to the University of Idaho. They wanted to see Matt Walsh and Michael Knowles, two conservative commentators (people who share their opinions on news and politics). The event was the last stop on Turning Point USA’s spring tour of college campuses. Turning Point USA, or TPUSA, is a group that talks about conservative ideas to young people. The tour had some problems earlier. At the University of Georgia, not many people showed up, and the crowd had low energy. The group’s CEO, Erika Kirk, even canceled her appearance there, saying she was worried about safety. Another event in Iowa was canceled too. But in Idaho, it was totally different. People started lining up hours before the event at the Idaho Central Credit Union Arena. The line wrapped around the building! Inside, the arena can hold over 4,000 people, but TPUSA only set up 869 seats. That meant more than 1,000 people couldn’t get in. “I’m very sorry for the over 1,000 people who did not make it in the room tonight,” Knowles told the crowd. Inside, volunteers handed out free hats and mini American flags. Walsh and Knowles answered questions and even debated with people who disagreed with them. Moes and Aten said TPUSA’s videos helped them explain their own views on topics like abortion. For them, the long drive was totally worth it.
why_it_matters: Even if you disagree with someone’s opinions, traveling far to hear them shows how strongly young people can feel about ideas, and that kind of passion shapes the world you will live in.

keywords.explanation: People who create and host online audio or video shows.
Experts who share their opinions on news and events, often in media.
Favoring traditional values and being cautious about big changes in society.
The chief executive officer, the top manager in charge of an organization.
Called off or stopped from happening.
Discussed a topic by arguing different sides of an issue.

questions.question: Who were the two speakers at the Idaho event?
Why did over 1,000 people not get into the arena?
options: Only 869 seats had been set up
questions.correct_answer: Only 869 seats had been set up

questions.question: What free items did volunteers hand out?
At which earlier event was the crowd described as having 'low energy'?
What topic did Moes and Aten say TPUSA helped them explain their views on?

background_read: Turning Point USA was started in 2012 by Charlie Kirk (a conservative activist) to spread his political views among students.
The group's campus tour often features well-known conservative commentators like Matt Walsh and Michael Knowles, who have shows on The Daily Wire (a conservative media company).
Idaho has a largely conservative population, so the event there attracted far bigger crowds than those in Georgia and Iowa.
Article_Structure: WHO: Fans Chloe Moes and Marissa Aten, and conservative commentators Matt Walsh and Michael Knowles.
WHAT: They attended the final campus event of Turning Point USA's spring tour, where over 1,000 people could not get in.
WHERE: At the University of Idaho's arena.
WHY: They were motivated by their conservative beliefs and the chance to see famous online personalities in person.

perspectives.description: They feel that TPUSA gives them a voice and helps them understand conservative viewpoints on issues like abortion, and they are willing to travel far to be part of it.
They believe TPUSA spreads divisive and sometimes extreme ideas, and worry that large, passionate crowds can drown out other viewpoints on campus.
They see the tour as a successful way to promote free speech and debate, and they highlighted the Idaho turnout as proof of growing interest.
